prompt: Defend a genuinely unpopular or unfashionable opinion that you actually hold. Not a strawman — something you'd stand by.
response:
I believe that almost all contemporary popular music—not just the chart hits, but the entire indie/alt/hip-hop/EDM ecosystem that self-identifies as “serious” or “innovative”—has been in a 25-year creative stall that is deeper and more sterile than the much-maligned disco era of the late 1970s. The slump is masked only by better production software, louder mastering, and an explosion of micro-genre branding that gives the illusion of forward motion.

1. Harmonic language has actually regressed.  
   Jazz and Western art music pushed tonality to its limits between 1900-1970; even chart pop once smuggled in ii-V-I turnarounds, modal interchange, and chromatic mediants. Today’s SoundCloud/Bandcamp “sophisticates” treat a borrowed iv or a Neapolitan substitution as a graduate-level revelation. The average lo-fi bedroom track has less harmonic ambition than a 1943 Rodgers & Hart refrain.

2. Rhythmic innovation peaked with Jungle and 2-step Garage (1994-2001).  
   Since then, the global default has grid-locked into halftime trap hi-hats or four-on-the-floor side-chained kicks. Even the polyrhythmic experiments of so-called “deconstructed club” are timbral rearrangements, not new rhythmic syntax; mathematically they’re still looping 4/4 or 6/8 with occasional tuplets. Compare that to the off-grid swing feel of 1996 drum-&-bass, where programmers hand-nudged 64th-note ghosts that created emergent 3:5 cross-rhythms against the master tempo.

3. Lyric writing has been throttled by self-aware “vibe” culture.  
   The last two decades reward understatement to the point of self-cancellation: songs that apologize for having a thesis. Direct statement is read as cringe, so every emotion is filtered through three layers of ironic hedging or ASMR mumble. The result is a linguistic flatness worse than the most cliché 1980s arena-rock—because at least those bands were shooting for a catastrophic grandeur instead of whispered ambiguity.

4. Technological abundance ≠ aesthetic progress.  
   Unlimited track counts, pitch correction, and sample packs have not produced a corresponding leap in compositional daring; they’ve only lowered the noise floor for mediocrity. The same DAW presets that let a teenager sound “radio ready” also foreclose surprise: every vocal sits at −6 LUFS, every 808 is tuned to the root, every stereo bus is widened with the same Mid/Side EQ curve. High craft once required resourceful mic placement, tape splices, and musicians who could execute odd-meter passages in real time; today it requires shopping the right splice pack.

5. The economic collapse of the middle-class career musician has dried up the apprenticeship pipeline.  
   In 1975 a working band could play six sets a night, five nights a week, reading charts on the fly. That reps-per-hour regime created the harmonic fluency underpinning Stevie Wonder, Steely Dan, or Weather Report. The current economy forces artists to be content creators first, musicians second; TikTok metrics reward 15-second hooks, not 64-bar modulating bridges. Technical facility is now a luxury hobby rather than a job requirement, and the music shows it.

6. “But there’s great stuff if you dig!” misses the point.  
   Scattershot exceptions do not refute a systemic stagnation. Yes, you can find Jacob Collier or Black Midi, just as 1978 had its Gentle Giant die-hards. The difference: in 1978 the marginal weirdos were still tethered to a mainstream that itself contained harmonic sophistication (see: Earth, Wind & Fire). Today’s progressive corner is quarantined; it has zero influence on the center of mass, which is mum-bled trap ballads sewn to 3-note pentatonic loops.

7. Nostalgia isn’t the objection—stasis is.  
   I don’t want another Beatles, I want the next unforeseen paradigm that makes the Beatles sound antique. Instead we get decade-long revivals of revivals: 2020s indie rehashing 2010s chillwave rehashing 1980s new-wave rehashing 1960s jangle. It’s turtles all the way down, each iteration scrubbed cleaner of the original’s idiosyncrasy.

So I’ll say it plainly: if you transported an average jazz-club listener from 1965 to the present and played them the most applauded “cutting-edge” playlist of 2024, their first reaction would not be “How futuristic!” but “Why did they stop?”
